codeReview.summary: The feature delivers the Traceability module, CRUD API, category/link validation, and init-time file creation, but it does not satisfy the concurrent-write requirement because writes are unsynchronized and can drop entries under contention.

issues.description: Concurrent writes use a read-modify-write cycle without locking (`read_all` at line 250 followed by overwrite in `persist`), so simultaneous writes can lose previously written entries (last-writer-wins). This conflicts with VAL-TRACE-006, which requires concurrent writes to preserve all entries.

The concurrency test explicitly permits lost entries (`length(raw["traceability"]) >= 1`) instead of asserting all concurrent writes are retained, so it validates weaker behavior than required by VAL-TRACE-006.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The elixir-backend skill prescribes TDD steps (write failing tests first), but this worker implemented production code before creating tests and still reported `followedProcedure: true`; this indicates the procedure or adherence checks are not aligned with actual execution expectations.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Transcript skeleton shows `Create lib/bropilot/traceability.ex` before `Create test/traceability_test.exs`, and no pre-implementation failing test run.

sharedStateObservations.observation: AGENTS.md says to use `Bropilot.Map.Store` for map data, but traceability implementation performs direct `File` + `Bropilot.Yaml` operations. The convention likely needs an explicit exception for traceability or clarification of when direct file I/O is acceptable.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: AGENTS.md coding convention: "Use `Bropilot.Map.Store` for map data"; implementation in `lib/bropilot/traceability.ex` reads/writes via `File.exists?/1`, `File.write/2`, and `File.rename/2`.

summary: Review failed due to a blocking concurrency correctness gap: concurrent write paths can drop entries, and tests codify that weaker behavior instead of enforcing full retention required by VAL-TRACE-006.
